title: Regulation of brain development and brain function by the transcriptional repressor RP58.
abstract: The mechanisms regulating the formation of the cerebral cortex have been well studied. In the developing cortex, (also known Znf238, Zfp238, and Zbtb18), which encodes a sequence-specific transcriptional repressor, is expressed in glutamatergic projection neurons and progenitor cells. Targeted deletion of Rp58 leads to dysplasia of the neocortex and hippocampus, a reduction in the number of mature cortical neurons, and defects in laminar organization due to abnormal neuronal migration within the cortical plate. During late embryogenesis, Rp58-deficient mice have larger numbers of progenitor cells due to a delay in cell cycle exit. RP58 represses all four Id genes (Id1-Id4), which regulate cell cycle exit in the developing cerebral cortex, and is essential for transcriptional repression of Ngn2 and Rnd2, which regulate the multipolar-to-bipolar transition during neuronal migration independently of its role in cell cycle exit.
